humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark uses humanities subjects both as sources of factual content and as domains requiring specialized, graduate-level analytical reasoning (such as translation of ancient scripts and linguistic analysis).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: A multimodal benchmark where 86% of the questions are text-only and approximately 14% combine text with an image reference.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates multi-step expert reasoning, factual recognition of specialized academic concepts, translation and structural analysis of historical languages, domain-specific symbolic reasoning, and exact-match verification.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is designed as a broad academic benchmark spanning dozens of subjects across STEM, humanities, and social sciences.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates models including GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking, o1, o3-mini, and DeepSeek-R1.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems evaluated to measure the gap between current AI capabilities and human expertise.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes ASJC areas such as Classics, Language and Linguistics, Philosophy, Literature, Visual Arts, Archaeology, Music, and History.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates models using Accuracy (%) and RMS Calibration Error (%).

published_after_march_2023.note: Preprint posted in early 2025 (arXiv ID 2501, though datestamped 2026 in PDF).

english_language.note: The paper and benchmark questions are in English, with some referencing non-English historical scripts.

summary: This paper introduces Humanity's Last Exam (HLE), a multi-modal benchmark of 2,500 highly challenging, expert-designed questions spanning STEM, humanities, and social sciences. HLE serves as a rigorous standard to assess frontier LLM capabilities on academic tasks as existing benchmarks saturate.
key_findings: State-of-the-art LLMs achieve very low accuracy on HLE, with the best-performing reasoning model (o3-mini high) reaching only 13.4% accuracy, while non-reasoning models score below 5%. Furthermore, all evaluated models exhibit high RMS calibration errors (above 70%), indicating a strong tendency to confidently hallucinate incorrect answers.
review_notes: Resolved disagreements: 1) 'publication_year' set to 2025 based on arXiv ID (2501) rather than the likely typographical '2026' datestamp in the PDF; 2) 'humanities_role' set to 'both' because while the dataset acts as a broad knowledge content evaluation, the questions inherently test domain-specific expert reasoning (e.g., linguistics, classics translation); 3) 'general_purpose_benchmark' set to true, favoring Cip's interpretation, as the benchmark aims for broad subject coverage across disciplines; 4) Merged both analysts' task lists to include both structural QA types and specific analytical processes evaluated.
